result:
There is a specific kind of magic that exists only in the hours before dawn. The world holds its breath. At four in the morning, the relentless demands of the day haven't yet begun to knock at the door, and the weight of expectations is still dormant. It is a liminal space, tucked neatly between the fading echoes of yesterday and the unwritten script of tomorrow. 

I like to sit by the window with a mug of coffee that is slightly too hot, watching the darkness slowly unspool. Outside, the streetlights cast long, amber tunnels through the fog, solitary beacons in a sleeping world. There is no traffic, no blaring sirens, no urgent emails—just the low hum of the refrigerator and the occasional creak of the house settling into its bones. In this quiet, my mind wanders without a leash, tracing the edges of half-forgotten dreams and contemplating the sheer absurdity of existence. 

Then comes the shift. It happens so gradually that if you blink, you miss the exact moment of change. The deep navy of the sky begins to fray at the edges, giving way to a fragile, bruised peach. A solitary bird tests its voice—a single, piercing note—and then falls silent, as if embarrassed by the audacity of breaking the quiet. Soon, the chorus will begin, the sun will crest the horizon, and the world will demand its due. But for now, in this stolen sliver of time, everything is perfectly still.
